Leviticus 22 is relentless in its demand for holiness, yet the relentlessness itself is the point. God does not casually permit His people to approach Him. When He repeatedly says "I am the LORD," He is not offering comfort but establishing the ground of His authority: He alone determines what holiness looks like, and His standards are non-negotiable. The chapter divides into two movements—who may eat the sacred offerings and what animals may be offered—but both circle back to a single obsession: protecting the integrity of worship. A priest with a skin disease cannot eat the offering. An animal with a crushed testicle cannot be sacrificed. These are not arbitrary rules born from squeamishness. They reflect a profound truth about what it means to approach the living God. When we draw near to Him, we cannot bring our brokenness, our disorders, our half-hearted compromises, and expect Him to receive them as though they were acceptable. The offerings must be whole. The offerer must be clean. God's holiness demands nothing less.

But here is where the chapter confronts us with its real weight: the law itself becomes a mirror showing us our condition. If a priest cannot approach God's offering while unclean, what does this mean for us? The chapter extends beyond Aaron's family to "all the Israelites" and even to "any foreign resident"—the laws are not elite requirements but universal standards. And yet no one comes to worship without the stains of the day clinging to them. No one brings an offering without moral blemish. The law exposes the gap between what God requires and what we can supply. This is not meant to drive us to despair alone, but to drive us toward understanding why redemption itself must come as a gift. The defective animal cannot be accepted; the unclean person cannot draw near. These prohibitions are not mere ritual stipulations—they are theological declarations about the distance between a holy God and unholy people.

Notice that God does not abolish the offering system when His people prove unable to keep themselves perpetually clean. Instead, He provides procedures: wash at evening, become clean, then eat. The system accommodates human limitation while maintaining God's holiness. There is grace here, but it is the grace of regulated access, not casual familiarity. Similarly, when someone eats the sacred offering in error, restitution is required—a fifth of the value, plus the offering itself returned to the priest. Even mistakes carry weight. This chapter assumes a world where holiness matters so much that carelessness in worship is not merely unfortunate but dangerous. We live in an age that has largely forgotten this. We approach God with our phones, our wandering thoughts, our routine prayers. But Leviticus 22 asks: Do we understand what it means to stand before Him at all?

The chapter concludes by bringing the theme home: "You must not profane My holy name. I must be acknowledged as holy among the Israelites." The regulations about offerings and priestly cleanness are not ends in themselves; they exist to declare God's holiness to the world. When Israel observes these laws, they proclaim to the nations that their God is set apart, transcendent, worthy of reverence. And when they break them—when they offer the defective, the unclean, the careless—they profane His name. This is what should strike us hardest: our worship either declares God's worth or diminishes it. There is no neutral ground. As we read these commands meant for ancient Israel, we face a question that refuses to be avoided: In our own worship, our own offerings of time and attention and devotion, are we honoring God's holiness, or are we profaning His name through our indifference?
